Byte values run from 0-255, not 1-256.  ASCII values are from 0-126, to keep it "7-bit safe," so the high-bit can be used for parity checking.  Printable ASCII characters begin at 65, with "A".

33:36 Correction: The lowest number of QR codes is a *tenth of,* not *ten times,* the number of legal chess positions. There are 5.7×10^45 QR codes (Version 1, lowest redundancy), compared to 4.5×10^46 chess positions.
Error at 11:05 into the video.  8 bit ASCII are not assigned a value from 1 to 256.  They are assigned a value from 0 to 255 giving them 256 possible combinations.  Zero is 00000000.  One is 00000001.  255 is 11111111.

QR codes aren't just a short code they are the entire URL. Equivalent to converting the URL into binary and then drawing the resulting 1s and 0s as black and white squares.  So as long as we don't "run out" of URLs we won't run out of QR codes.
QR codes are hella dangerous.  You NEED a QR code reader to translate them, and you can't see what's in them until you have activated them.  A QR code with a malicious link in it, built to be a MITM malicious attack is more or less undetectable.

Minor correction around 11:10. ASCII (the modern version) is is indeed 8 bits but the range is 0 - 255, not 1 - 256. You can verify the maximum value of n unsigned bits with the formula 2^n - 1 (0 will always be the minimum value for unsigned bits).

To claim that "ASCII has its roots in Morse code" is kind of misleading. They are _fundamentally_ different in very important ways. ASCII is a fixed-length binary encoding. Morse code is a variable-length "sort of binary" (originally closer to ternary) encoding.

11:16 Correction, ASCII was originally and kinda still today a 7-bit charecter set. 'Extended ASCII' is 8 bit. But the original and actual ASCII set is only 128 characters or 7-bit, even if it is usually stored in a byte format, the MSbit is simply just ignored.

11:11 Correction: byte encoding doesn't use ASCII, it uses ISO 8859-1, which is a superset of ASCII. ASCII only uses numbers from 0 to 127, ISO 8859-1 uses numbers from 0 to 255 (not 1 to 256, so 'a' is indeed 97, not 98), leaving the first 0-127 the same as ASCII.

2:49 Morse did not use letters; he invented only digits. The digits were then grouped in groups of five, and finally, the combined number was used to read the correct word associated with that number from the dictionary. Letters were invented by other people years after.

Morse didn't send long and short pulses as his system could only click the contact open and shut -- not vibrate. Instead, the short and long were the spaces BETWEEN the clicks. It was only later when we had radio and crude speakers that you could send long and short sounds.

Actually: ASCII holds only 128 reliably encodable values! The remaining range is code point-dependent which makes it internationally unsuitable. Also the first handful of ASCII-values, as well as the last one, are unprintable so they aren't characters in the vernacular sense.
Actually, ASCII only uses 7 bits, encoding values from 0 up to 127. Serial transmission using (nowadays legacy) standards like RS232 usually only transferred those 7 bits. However, 8 bits turned out to be easier to handle for computers, and it's the standard computer settled.

Correction: Strictly speaking, ASCII is only 7 bit, not 8 bit. ASCII is 0 through 127 (128 code points in total). Numerous other character sets have extended ASCII for utilizing 8 bits and beyond. Without getting into the details, we often use UTF-8 nowadays, which is a superset of ASCII.

Trivia nitpick: The distress code is not SOS. SOS would be ".../_ _ _/..." However, the distress code is "... _ _ _ ..." -- that is, there are no pauses between sections! This is usually represented by placing an overbar above the letters, to indicate that the operator should not insert pauses.

SOS stands for Save Our Ship, which the morse code operators were at first required to type out fully, but they soon noticed it was the only thing in their codes that could be cut down to SOS, and that it was very memorizable, thus it Quickly became the go to shorthand for any disaster situation

In the 1990's, David Hecht at Xerox Research developed an encoding called dataglyphs, using only diagonal slashes that can both encode data and be used for grayscale imaging. A kind of steganography. Much more attractive but perhaps a little harder to decode than QR, which are indeed pretty ugly.
Didn’t Hiro first start out with recognising a black square with a pattern in it. The vision algorithm then recognised the square transformed it through x,y, z to flat on to the camera so the pattern inside the rectangle could be matched. QR codes were then developed from this original algorithm.

Comparing a UPC bar code to a QR code isn't really fair, because the UPC bar code is both an encoding (the bar code) and a schema for assigning identifiers to products (the UPC number). If we used QR codes for UPCs, while there would be a lot more options, we still have the same problem fundamentally.

at 6:44 it should be added that When a barcode is scanned, the system calculates what the last digit should be based on the other numbers. If the calculated last digit matches the one in the barcode, it means the scan is likely correct. If it doesn’t match, there might be a problem, like a mistake or damage.

correction: ASCII uses 7 bits and only has 128 characters. In Unicode the leading bit is used to indicate a new character (0 if it's the start of a new character, 1 if the byte is a continuation of a character). Since all ASCII characters use 1 byte, the leading bit is always a 0 in each byte of an ASCII message
QR codes are great and always have been, the issue for a long time was implementation. QR codes were functionally useless until IOS/Android added built in scanning to their respective camera apps. Before that you needed a dedicated scanning app which made the codes completely worthless for 99% of the population.

A key point of SOS is also that it is not tapped out as the word SOS, which would have pauses between each letter, but as one single full sequence with no inter-letter pausing. This is usually denoted by an overline over the whole sequence. Just as equally it could be EEETTTEEE or even VZE again all with overline.

ASCII isn't based on morse code, just because it converts to a number and then that number represents binary doesn't mean it's morse code.  ASCII is just a character encoding, UTF-8 and all the other encodings are the same. A character is represented by some number, and computers operate in binary. No morse code involved.

3:40 -- Not only doesn't S.O.S. stand for anything, the international distress code isn't even a sequence of the three letters "S", "O", and "S". When sending "SOS" the morse code will be "... --- ...", with short pauses between the letters. The distress signal is "...---...", without the pauses, so it's actually a single symbol.

Nitpicking and not relevant for the rest of the story, but Ascii goes in theory only to 127. UTF-8 extends it, using the first MSB to encode how many bytes are used for the glyph. This results in Ascii not being able ro use the first bit. If this is 0, the glyph consist of 1 byte. So in the 0->127 range, UTF-8 and Ascii are equal.

34:08 Honestly, I thought QR Code stood for "Quadrilateral Read": The info is contained in a square, BUT the info can be read from all sorts of irregular angles, where the QR code appears to be some other arbitrary quadrilateral (like a kite or trapezoid). Ergo, it can _read_ any _quadrilateral_ that is formatted into the correct code.
SOS is more than just “easy to send”. It follows the Rule of Three (three consecutive or simultaneous signals, such as whistle blasts or smoke columns, means whoever sent them is in need of rescue). S is three dots, O is three dashes, and the whole signal is three letters.  It also just so happens to be a backronym of “save our souls”.

Small correction should be added. ASCII was designed to be used with 7 bits. This was due to when it was designed 8 bits (a byte) was not really as common, and in-fact it was unnecessary as 7 bits encoded everything needed just fine. So saying ASCII is 8 bits while works for the QR code, it is a bit misleading, since 8th bit will be always 0.

11:11 One byte represents a number from 0 to 255, not 256. And ASCII code in itself was at first defined with codes from 0 to 127, actually, so 7 bits were enough, in "modern" computers, character coding was extended to use full 8 bits, leading to regional set of characters, but it was not strict ASCII anymore, ASCII was part of it. Just to be precise.

EAN-13 (European style barcode) has a more interesting encoding than that of UPC. In fact, if we look at the bars, it appears that there are 12 digits. The 13th digit is derived from the six digits on the left. Extended ASCII is not 1 to 256, it's zero to 255, for a _total_ of 256 characters, zero being the NUL character. A very common mistake, but 256 is the first number that requires 9 bits.  I'll leave aside the difference between the original ASCII being 7-bit and the Extended ASCII being 8-bit because Extended ASCII itself is another complex topic entirely.
An excellent installment!  I find it interesting that the registration marks 101010 in binary are 42 in the ASCII table and 52 in octal. This equates to the symbol *, and if you count each bit that makes up each of the registration marks, you get 6, 6, 6—six bits per mark with three marks per QR code.  Why was this scheme chosen for the registration marks?

It is interesting to me that, when IBM asked MIT engineers to comment on the original design of the UPC, they said the UPC would be temporary at best, because optical character recognition would soon supplant barcodes. The UPC proved so successful, development on OCR all but stopped. QR codes show that smart symbol coding still beats OCR handily, 50 years later.
I wrote the first smart phone application that was designed specifically for use in space, on the International Space Station, flown on the first iPhones sent to the ISS in 2010.  I used a QR code to optically determine attitude and distance from a fixed location.  I always wondered if the people, or person, that came up with the QR code knew of how it was used.
The OCR software makers allowed QR codes to take over what should have been their sole market. @Veritasium Minor correction: EAN13's check digit is just that, a digit to detect if the rest of the code has been read correctly. It can handle 1 digit errors but not two or more. But it can't calculate what is wrongly read and correct it(not even one digit) like said in the video. That would require some kind of ECC (Error correcting code) with would require more digits.

The letters "SOS" do not actually stand for anything specific; they were chosen for their simplicity and ease of transmission. While many people associate it with phrases like "Save Our Souls" or "Save Our Ship," these are backronyms created after the fact. The original intent was simply to have a distinctive signal that could be easily recognized and transmitted in emergencies

•	S: Three short dots (•••) 	•	O: Three long dashes (–––) 	•	S: Three short dots (•••)  Originally, S.O.S. was selected because it’s an easy-to-remember pattern in Morse code, not because it stood for any particular words. Over time, it’s become widely associated with phrases like “Save Our Souls” or “Save Our Ship,” although these meanings were assigned after the code was established.

12:50 You miss an important feature of the square patterns: if you scan a _rotated_ camera picture line by line, _no matter what angle_ , the processor can easily detect the pattern 010111010. When it finds three of these positions in the picture, it has a clue that this might be a QR code, and it already knows its orientation, even distortion, and can re-calculate the original code picture.

S.O.S has other symbolism in that it's a trio of 3's... Three 3's.  Triangles are used for warning symbols pretty much universially across the globe for this reason as well, three sides and three corners.  Three sides and three corners correlates directly to three shorts and three longs.  In effect, S.O.S. is a codified triangle.  The idea that "trouble comes in 3's" though... is quite ancient.
The video is missing the future of QR Code which is about to roll out by GS1. GS1 is the organization behind the GTIN Number, basically the number in the barcode on pretty much all products in supermarkets around the world. And they are changing the standard from the barcode to a QR Code. Every product will have lost of new information and even different information based on location and device

The "SOS" morse you sent as an exaple was wrong. For those that are convinced that SOS is an acronym a quick Wikipedia search explains that, originally (in 1906), SOS was chosen because is easy to remember and to read. The idea that it is an acronym for "save our souls" or even "save our ship" emerged years later as a way to help in remembering it. Fun fact, this phenomenon in which a meaning is invented for a sequence of letter is called a backronym

In short: - Three position squares allow for the computer to orient the code properly, even when looking at an angle - One alignment square to rescale the code - Two timing strips that connect the position squares, allow for version determination - Formatstrips for parameters - Four bits to specify the data format - Bites stored in binary - Error correction code - masking to make it more easily reasable

The first Mars rover had regularly spaced holes in the wheels.  As it moved on the sand the holes formed morse code characters in the tracks.  It was Morse Code J P L for Jet propulsion Laboratories.. Thousands of radio Amateurs still use morse code, just for fun.  At around 30 words per minute skill level,, nobody bothers to write it down, they just hear it as a living language.  The brain process it as you read a book.

A thought about the 3 large squares and the single small square used to determine the orientation of the entire code:  It occurs to me that if only one corner had a large square that it would serve the same purpose as 3 but would require more aspect data to distinguish.  If only the upper left corner had a large square, and you included 2 small squares instead of only one, you'd have more space for data.  Does that make sense?
5:49 As someone who works in the industry, it goes a little further than this. Every product has its own barcode, but also very small changes in a product means that it gets a completely new barcode. Did a product change its label to be a little more colorful? That gets a new barcode. Change the size from 10oz to 9.8oz? New barcode. Change the vintage on a $20 bottle of wine? New barcode. That's how detailed this process gets.

In the world of retail sales there are primarily two barcode formats used, UPC and EAN. The UPC format is 12 digits while the EAN is 13. These two formats are predominantly used in their own regions, the UPC is used ONLY  in the US and Canada, while the EAN is used EVERYWHERE else globally. : )  Nice video, but the part about the Barcode is misleading... Also the standard Go game board is not 21x21 , but 19x19 which was not mentioned.
31:48 reminds me of the issues with the MD5 hashing algorithm, more specifically the fact that older hashing algorithms are no longer used for sensitive data  Shortly after that point in the video, Mashiro mentions adding encryption to QR codes. What we call Morse Code should more accurately be described as Vail Code.  Morse's original design had three different symbols; a dot, a short dash, and a long dash, and the timings between them mattered a lot more.  Vail simplified it down to two symbols and reorganized things so that more common letters took way less time to send, a thing Morse tried to do but didn't really emphasize.  It's a bit annoying we still call it Morse Code.

Morse code (called "carrier wave" or "CW") communication, is STILL employed by many amateur radio operators.  Proficiency in its use used to be required for obtaining a ham license, but that's no longer the case.  But some hams, simply, LIKE it.  But there's a more practical reason for its continued use.  When radio propagation conditions are really poor, CW can often provide reliable communication, while voice modulated signals can't get through.

I remember first seeing QR codes in widespread use in Japan back in 2008.  That made sense, as their cell phones were a lot better (relative to western flip phones of the era) and data plans more ubiquitous, but typing in a URL on a flip phone would still have been pretty tedious.  It took a while longer for them to catch on here, even after smartphones became a thing, mostly because, for the longest while, you still needed a 3rd party app to read them.
you go the meaning of SOS slightly wrong it actually does mean something just not because of the letters that were assigned to it.  dating way back even before the first lighthouses, 3 is a special value to its the shortest signal you can send where the likelihood of it being random noise is able to be decerned by timing. so 3 of anything with similar timings becomes a warning something like a candle signal during a war became a pattern of 3 repetition.
I love QR codes but Aztec codes are superior and should be given more attention. They were largely held back because the open source library that like 90% of apps relied on for barcode reading didn't support them but this was fixed a long time ago. At this point, they're effectively just as widely supported but nearly as commonly used. They have all the benefits of QR codes but with even more improvements for the size like not even needing a quiet zone.
If you want something quick to use then QR codes are great, because they don't take up much space, they don't involve you typing in anything, they are reliably readable by a camera, unlike text, and they are recognisable as a code to scan with a camera. They are also pretty each to create and scale to the available space you have for them. They are great to link different types of media too, such as printed construction drawings and 3D models or renders.

Small correction: common ASCII is actually 7 bits (0-127), not 8. There are variations, but they are not universal. UTF-8 is another encoding, and it actually uses 1-4 bytes per symbol. 1 byte for ascii, where 7 bits are the symbol itself, and the highest bit is for letting computer know that it is an ascii character. Different combinations of highest bits let computer know how many bytes current symbol takes. If I remember correctly, poop emoji is 4 bytes :)

One cool thing you can do with QR codes that intend to contain proprietary data, such as an inventory tracking app, is encode the data as a parameter to a URL. The app will know to expect that URL to say “these are the codes we are looking for” but also as a way for a generic code scanner to redirect any given code to an App Store to download the correct app. Of course this introduces some privacy concerns but something like a container ID may be harmless to send.

I used to do LTL truck driving and pick up at the DENSO facility in Athens, Tennessee daily. Our shipping label books were comprised of quad-duplicated color coded QR sticker sets (yellow, blue, green, red, etc.) for each pallet that belonged to a shipment. We scanned them with a Honeywell handheld device.  The extreme irony in our business being centralized around shipping freight using a QR code system and picking up from DENSO daily has fallen upon me. Thanks for this.
6:18 Actually, there’s a misunderstanding about UPC-A barcodes in the video. There are only 100 billion possible combinations, not 1 trillion. Of those, 6 digits are for the brand, allowing for about 1 million brands, and 5 digits are for products, giving each brand around 100,000 unique products. This means we won't utilize all 100 billion barcodes unless every brand uses all its assigned codes. So, it's entirely possible to run out of unique barcodes if demand increases.
There is a bit of the story missing. Railroads employed a version of the barcode on the side of freight cars where different colors and patterns identified the railroad, car type, and an identification number. Track-side scanners would read the codes and help the railroad keep tabs on where equipment was. Look up KarTrack. It was used in North America between 1967 and 1977. While it as since been replaced by RFID tags, it is still possible to see barcodes on older equipment.

24:24 Derek: Set each coefficient to be a variable, set the polynomial equal to zero and solve for x=1. Then repeat for x=2 and, if the results are different, you know no error occurred. Me: OK, I'm with you so far. Derek: Do this for all the coefficients and, where the error occurred, the two results are the same. Me: Huh, that's really neat. Derek: ...and they're both equal to the original value. Me: What the hell, that's sorcery. Mathematical dark magic. Get back, numerical Satan!
QR codes can also carry a hidden message (payload) that is hard/impossible to extract or even detect by normal reader. This allows for transferring messages hidden literally in plain sight, also known as steganography. It is a kind of security through obscurity not encountered by people livign in "walled gardens" of the world, but absolutely essential for e.g. journalists and human rights activists working in hostile environments, where freedom of speech and transaction is restricted.
Actually, the first Morse code for SOS was CQD ( ▄▄▄ ▄ ▄▄▄ ▄   ▄▄▄ ▄▄▄ ▄ ▄▄▄   ▄▄▄ ▄ ▄ ) which was for Maritime transmission (a Marconi initiated standard) for "Come Quickly, Distress". I believe because of the Morse sequencing it was simplified to SOS which did have the meaning of "Save Our Ship". However, reading up on it, the SOS is meant to be it's OWN morse code of SOS, with an overbar, to distinguish it from the three individual letters. It's meant to be a 9-pulse SINGLE letter.

One minor nit: at the beginning, Morse code receiving equipment did not emit tones, but just clicks, one at the beginning and one at the end of the pulse. Operators learned to distinguish these, and were able to decode just by sound. But, the video, like nearly everything else on the topic, overlays the sound of toned pulses. Morse code was used extensively for at least 70 years before it became more common to have the receiver hear tones, mostly because of CW transmission of the signals.
Indonesians are also really into using QR codes. They’ve even standardized QR payments with something called QRIS (with IS standing for "Indonesia Standard"; also a pun of keris, a traditional Indonesian weapon). What’s craaaazzyy about QRIS is that it accepts payments virtually from any bank and any e-wallet. At first, each payment provider had its own QR code, but now it's just QRIS everywhere. From minimarkets to restaurants, and even street vendors/peddlers on the roadside! It’s wild!
Summary in case you came here just for the answer: The concentric squares in the corner help your QR reader determine the orientation & scale of the code, the zebra crossings help the phone figure out the timing of positions, and most of the rest is data. You'll miss out on some of the history of the topic by the way, including the origin of Morse code. cool qr code fact: it just needs to be 'dark' and 'light' permitting you to use variations other than black and white, for example, a light blue foreground and dark grey background. in practice, only 64 characters or so is practical for normal size qr codes when scanning (making them pretty ideal for urls). also, UPC code's first section is the vendor, so it's not as simple as 10^12 possibilities as mentioned -- your limited to 10^6 vendors which can each have 10^4 products (per 10 country codes).

7:33 While there are 100 billion combinations of digits in the bar code, as explained here, that's not really how the limit would work. If it splits into vendor on one side and product code on the other side, the real limits are 1 million vendors with up to 100,000 products each. I suspect, if it's a "universal" code used worldwide, the bar codes would hit the vendor limit before they hit the product limit. But I can see some vendors hitting the product limit if they sell lots of variations of small things.

Morse dispatches a telegraph message from the U.S. Capitol to Alfred Vail at a railroad station in Baltimore, Maryland. The message—“What Hath God Wrought?”—was telegraphed back to the Capitol a moment later by Vail. S.O.S. is an internationally recognized distress signal. Originally, it was adopted for maritime use in 1906 to communicate emergencies, especially at sea. Contrary to popular belief, it doesn’t officially stand for anything like “Save Our Ship” or “Save Our Souls,” though those phrases have become associated with it over time. The signal was chosen because of its simplicity: in Morse code, it’s represented by three dots, three dashes, and three dots (···—···), making it easy to transmit and recognize. Today, it’s used as a general signal for help.
I needed to write macros out of necessity of my early-days engineering job.   Few years ago I wrote one to create QR code with black and white cells in Excel. I thought it was simple because I did a barcode before.   How was I wrong! I ended up spending a month! Of course all the math was a part of it but the Error Correction feature makes it harder ironically, because when there is an error in your code, at times the code still works. So it was a challenge checking where the error was.   I ended up having to do a lot of manual recheck by 'dot' by hand!

is it possible to put more QR codes one over another by coding with different colors? QR uses black and white as 1 and 0 but since white is made of red blue and yellow you can have up to 5 different value for pixel:  black: 0 red: 1 blue: 2 yellow: 3 white: 4  If you want even more values for pixel you can use secondary colors:  black: 0 red: 1 purple: 2 blue: 3 green: 4 yellow: 5 orange: 6 white: 7  With those you can have 3 bits for pixel. You can put 3 QRcodes in one, the parts of the code used for the scan could be shared while the rest is for the differents codes

His talk of authentication and validation that a code is legitimate is similar to the concept of SSL certificates, as is the notion of chain of trust. Second is encrypted data in a code that can only be decoded by those with the proper cypher, again, just like encrypted communications on web pages. However, the problem is always with issuance and abuse, that illegitimate actors can issue, for a short time, a valid code. We still struggle with this with email phishing and spoofing.  This particular area of security goes quite deep, but will become more important as codes continue to proliferate.

Huffman encoding could compress the data before it is encoded in a QR code.  Another way to increase the storage would be to increase the number of colors for example 256 colors like in the GIF format. Cameras can already scan in color.  If the scanned 24 bit color does not match the 8 bit color, the Pythagoras algorithm could find the 8 bit color closest to the 24 bit color by taking the 8 bit color that's closest to the 24 bit color scanned by measuring the distance as  ((R1-R2)^2 + (G1-G2)^2 + (B1-B2)^2) EXP(1/2).   Where R,G,B are the individual RGB values and 1 and 2 refer to the two colors being compared.

11:10 You meant to say "ASCII assigns every character a number from 0 to 255", not "1 to 256". But even that is not correct. ASCII assigns a number from 0 to 127. ASCII is a 7-bit code, not 8-bit. At the time it was invented, the notion of an 8-bit byte was not ubiquitous. Since then, 8-bit bytes did become ubiquitous and ASCII was almost universally transmitted as an 8-bit stream with the most significant bit just set to zero, and many encoding standards were developed that place ASCII at the first 128 code points out of 256 (or more), but they all differ in those last 128 characters, and are definitely not ASCII.

I'm not a computer scientist, but a materials scientist. In a roundabout way, the error correction kind of reminds me of crystallographic vectors in hexagonal space. Let's look at the miller indices (hkil), where i=-(h+k). This is a method of transforming the cubic (hkl) into a shape that is more compatible with hexagonal symmetry. You now have plane sets that are not equivalent in cubic space represented in hexagonal space where they are, indeed, equivalent. Obviously what I deal with is far simpler than any hypercubic or hyperhexagonal system, but it still felt cool to look at something like this and go, "wait, that seems kind of familiar"

I first saw the barcode of the USSR in the 70s of the last century.   These were metal frames with metal strips attached to them. These frames were mounted on the roof of trolleybuses and buses. And in certain places of the city, a light source was attached to lampposts, and a sensor receiving light was attached to another pillar. When the trolleybus passed, the lantern shone through the frame, and the metal plates were like a barcode. And the signal went to a primitive computer at the point of traffic controllers, and showed which trolleybus had passed, and how it corresponded to the traffic schedule.  Each trolleybus was assigned its own code.

Didn't know about the story with the universally black bit, and it is so nice hearing it from the man himself. It got me thinking: how much extra printer ink was wasted on printing that one extra black bit, over the billions and billions of QR codes printed? With the size at which some QR codes are printed, it must be quite significant, but I don't want to begin to estimate how much exactly.  Then on the other hand, if you think about the amount of QR codes on digital screens, there the black pixel actually saves resources, as a black pixel is cheaper than white to display.  So, does the black bit save or waste money? Is there even a way to know?
So there are one-dimensional codes such as barcodes.  And then there are two-dimensional codes such as QR codes.  We specifically teach people the difference because, for example, in the pharmaceutical industry, we use 2D codes to contain information about the drug package such as the drug name, the lot number, the expiration date, etc.  However, because QR codes are the most famous 2D codes, everyone incorrectly labels every 2D code a QR code.  The pharmaceutical one is the GS1 DataMatrix which superficially looks like the QR code because it's a 2D code, but if you look closer, it's very different.  The telltale sign of a QR code is it has 3 big squares.

It does not "identify every single grocery item you ever bought." Well, it does, but this could be clearer: it identifies every variant/version of item, not every item. If you buy another one of the same item again tomorrow, it has the same code. If you buy ten identical jars of peanut butter because you like that particular peanut butter so much, they have the exact same code. However, if you buy ten jars of peanut butter made by ten different manufacturers because you want to try them all, they will have ten different bar codes. If you buy five smooth and five crunchy from the same manufacturer, you will have five with one code, and five with another code.

What I always found fascinating were QArt codes. First one is a generator called "QArt Coder" or something similar. That one used an url shortener to ensure that specific letters were at specific portions of the QR code and just ignored error correction (at least I think that's how it works) so the generated code&error correction actually produced an image, while still being scanable. the second one is "Quick QR Art", an AI that generates images with bright/dark spots so it's scannable as a qr code, and while some of  the results are clearly just qr codes, some are so good you wouldn't know if you didn't expect it. (and sometimes they're simply not scannable, but hey)
26:26 Finite prime / Galois fields are also used for many forms of encryption ciphers. Among the popular are Advanced Encryption Standard & Elliptic Curve Cryptography and key exchange.  Sadly most implementations are re-using cryptographic parameters and thus breaking security. Many problems/solutions, like in Error Correcting QR-codes, can be pregenerated and mapped to input data in specific Finite prime / Galois fields.  This is an excellent topic for this Veritasium channel to cover, considering all Bitcoin security relies on this type of mathematics. (I am certain the world's all bitcoins will likely be stolen or suffer a market crash within a decade because of this)

Standard barcodes on products are not necessarily registered. Despite the different organizations involved in this process, there are a lot of duplicates. It’s trivial that some products from other countries may have randomly generated numbers) Plus internal goods that are needed to operate scales with a receipt or for products packaged in a store. So instead, each large network like Walmart or Amazon introduces its own personal barcodes so as not to depend on monopolistic organizations, which costs a lot of money for unnecessary standardization (although simply by increasing the number of lines, you can generate unique UUID-style numbers - and you won't have to pay anyone)

Correction on 11:04  1-256 is not the correct range, it's 0-225. Also ASCII only assigned from 0-127, which is 128 numbers, 7 bits.  Computers these days generally store data in groups of 8 bits, so the last bit was just wasted.  The 8th bit went un-used. Exteded ASCII used all 8, but it didn't take off.  Instead, Unicode assigned numbers to a looooot of non-english characters, chinese, japanese, arab, etc, and a variable length encoding like UTF-8 is used.  UTF-8 is backwards compatible with ASCII, the unused bit means something in UTF-8, and is set to 0 for all short characters.   If you want to know how UTF-8 works, computerphile has a great video on it featuring Tom Scott, it talks about UTF-8 and Unicode.
Why no attention for the more dark side of scanning QR codes.  Every time a user scan a QR code data is generated and send. – Time of scan: the time and date the QR code was scanned – Location of scan: device’s GPS coordinates – Type of device used: smartphone or tablet – User behavior: how user interacted with the content that was accessed after scanning the QR code – Demographics: information about the user’s age, gender, and other demographics –The codes user scanned before etc And all on a business self regulating own privacy policy that differ greatly around the world.  Qr code data has become big business and will be added to what is already known of you.  Expect that 97% of qr codes to be tracking codes.

There's a reason why QR codes work well with cameras while barcodes don't: In QR, you only need to distinguish between a black square and a white square. As long as you can see the squares, you can read the code. A relatively low 2D resolution works just fine. In barcode, there are 4 different thicknesses of lines. You need a pretty high resolution image of the barcode, to differentiate between width 3 and 4. The second dimension of your camera isn't any helpful in that. But a laser scanner with its fine laser dot can scan the code with a very high 1D resolution. Even a moving item is no problem for the laser scanner, it just shortens or widens the 1D image slightly, while a camera has to struggle with motion blur.

I was part of an imaging and print quality design group in a color printer and copier company. 20+ years ago we created color codes that could be printed on a page. These codes contained information about how we created the print or copy as well as other useful information about the state of the machine at the time the page was created. When scanning a print or copy to analyze image quality characteristics, the information color code was also scanned and decoded. While up to 16.7 million colors are typically available, the practical number that could be used for multicolor QR codes is much a smaller number. However, when compared to a binary, i.e. 2 color format, a multicolor format could provide much higher information density.

Small thing... but the barcodes @5:47 look like the 13-digit EAN-13 (European Article Number) as opposed to the 12-digit UPC. Same technology... but just in case someone is wondering why look different... it was what Europe adopted. You can convert any US UPC-A to an EAN by adding a leading 0 (this is the region code for US/Canada) and it doesn't alter the check digit calculation, though I believe that non-printed character is encoded into the UPC-A barcode as well...   This means that technically the so-called 12-digit North American UPC is actually a 13-digit code... as it includes the leading 0. Machines read the leading 0 but when we print the label, we omit it on the packaging.    The global  standard these days is the 14-digit GTIN

...and this video uses the incorrect SOS signal.  The thing about SOS is that it is NOT the letters S, O, and S. It is not ••• ——— •••, but rather •••———•••. Can you tell the difference? There are no spaces in between the dits and the dahs. SOS is just a mnemonic to remember the code. It might as well be EEETTTEEE, IWB, VZE... because once you remove the spaces between letters, like you are supposed to do with SOS, they all become the same exact signal.  The incorrect SOS is so ubiquitous, unfortunately, that  no online Morse code encoder/decoder (that I could find) can generate nor decode it.  If you go on English language  Wikipedia, at least the audio at the top of the article is correct, but then it uses the wrong code for the light-based version.

QR codes (Quick Response codes) are two-dimensional barcodes that can store a large amount of data, including URLs, text, or other information. They were first developed in 1994 by the Japanese company Denso Wave, originally for tracking parts in the automotive industry. Unlike traditional barcodes, which are one-dimensional and store data horizontally, QR codes store information both horizontally and vertically, allowing them to hold much more data.  QR codes can be scanned using a smartphone or tablet with a camera, making them widely used in marketing, payment systems, ticketing, and more. They have become particularly popular due to their ease of use and ability to instantly link users to websites, apps, or services without needing to type in information manually.

That being said, the prospect of being able to encode data in hexadecimal format with a 16 pigment standard would be very exciting.

Nice video V.  Always nice to hear someone acknowledge Alfred Vail (a.k.a 'Uncle Alfred') in the creation of Morse Code.  His most important contribution (and still unknown to most if not all 140 years later in 2024) was in creating a binary audio comms protocol that most importantly could be easily understood by the human brain when messages are sent/received.   It could be argued that this human 'perceptibility' is what allowed the protocol to actually be useful in remote comms, due to the speed at which messages could be created and deciphered, and hence to become as widely used as quickly as it did.   The student of history will no doubt be intrigued by this.  As a hint: It became like 'music to the ears'.  (Think of a waltz, 1-2-3, 2-2-3, 3-2-3, etc.)  Cheers all :-)

3:40 To expand on this: in Morse code, there is a short gap between letters. This is necessary because otherwise, the division between them is ambiguous. (There are also longer gaps between words, as compared to modern codes where space it treated as its own character.) So, three dots, pause, three dashes, pause, three dots, does indeed encode the letters "SOS". By contrast, the true SOS code is just three dots, three dashes, three dots, with no inter-letter gaps, unlike what the video shows here. It's called "SOS" simply because that's one possible way to interpret it if you were to impose letter divisions on it. The standard way to notate SOS, and other such "prosigns", in text is with a bar over the letters, but of course I can't do that here (at least, not that I know of).
Keep in mind qr codes can run code without even you knowing or noticing. Everywhere you have them- some special loyalty programs in shops, quick links in ads, some condensed info prompt, etc, etc.. Any one of those can be compromised. You would still see the "innocent" content, but the scan itself could include some routines, where any possible function of your device could be hijacked.   Malware could also be injected,  the device could also be asked to do stuff against your will (like share your location, your contacts, your cache, your notes, email, etc....)  QR Codes are very easy to modify and distribute.  Imagine you want to take a selfie or some photos of your friends and it just so happens a compromised qr code is in frame (and your phone auto scans it) well.... you're sol😅

One of the variations of the encoding dot's and dashes can be heard at DisneyLand at the railroad terminals, where yow will hear 'clicks ' that represent the dots and dashes. As a Click for a dot, and a dash are effectively indistinguishable, the dash is represented by 2 very closely placed clicks, or circuit closings. This may cause some confusion on high speed telegraphy. Fortunately most of those are handled by the conversion to turning on and off an oscillator, or what is called Continuous Wave, or CW.  There have also been a couple of different encodings for Morse code, both as international vs. domestic for various countries. We have mostly switched to the International encoding, but if you have access to an encyclopedia from the 40's or 50s, you might find an 'O' encoded as --, rather than ---, for example.
To those skilled, and experienced, in listening to Morse Code, much of the more common words begin to sound like mumbled English. The operator does not hear —  ••••  • (Dash DotDotDotDot Dot) but rather hears a badly spoken "the". In fact they can recognize different voices speaking, e.g. during World War II, the U.S. Army shortwave operators in Brisbane could recognize the particular Naval shortwave operator in Honolulu, by the distinctive cadence of their keying Morse Code. Veritasium skipped over it very quickly, but the time it takes to send an English text in Morse Code is at the minimum possible, because the most common letters in written English are given codes that take the _least_ time to transmit, e.g. E is one time-unit     T & I are both 3 time-units    A N & S are 5 time-units    D H M R U are 7 time-units.

I work at a grocery store and deal with barcodes all day long, hundreds of times a day. I often use QR codes for payment, and I also scan other qr codes sometimes. The main safety precaution is to scan with the right app. As in, if you want to pay - use the payment qr scanner in your bank app. If you want to load/sync a physical cheque for a transaction - use that menu in you bank app. If you want just a string - read it wit ha dedicated qr scanning app, and do not "open in app" before manually observing the link. The smartphone qr scanner can scan a code and send you immediately to the app without showing you the contents of the qr code, which is a shame, in my opinion, and why I have a dedicated qr scanning app. But also the bank apps and such all require confirmations of transaction after scanning the code. QR code is not at fault for phishing and fraud, the improperly designed systems (e.g. scanner apps, bank apps, etc) are.

This is an interesting dive into the structure of the QR code symbology. There are many more out there. There are linear symbologies like UPC (Universal Product Code, and it's sibling, EAN, European Article Number), Interleaved 2 of 5, Code 39, Code 128 and a host of others. There are two-dimensional (2D) symbologies like QR code, Data Matrix, DotCode, Aztec, Maxicode (used by United Parcel Service). There are Stacked symbologies like Code 49 and PDF417 (kind of two-dimensional but can still be read by a laser scanner if it's oriented correctly). There are symbologies that use color such as KarTrak. There are secret codes, such as Data Glyph and Digimarc (they look like background noise but really encode data). There are even symbologies that use bumps or ridges or slits. The point of ALL of these is simple: Capture data automatically with minimal errors.

Why not add +color layering to QR codes in the Z direction by using CYM additive color rules to add either; Hamming distance, for error correction, *or to triple data storage for the same area!  EXAMPLE BELOW 👇  If the layering is represented;  -bottom: magenta,  -middle: yellow, and -top: cyan.   __Rules for Combined colors__;  all 0s/no color      = white (W) magenta+ yellow= orange (ϴ),  yellow + cyan.      = green (G) cyan+ magenta.  = purple (P) mag+yellow+cyan= black (B)  ⁞⁞⁞⁞example data⁞⁞⁞⁞  Magenta = 101 110 001  ‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥(M0M MM0 00M) Yellow     = 101 001 010 ‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥(Y0Y 00Y 0Y0) Cyan       = 100 100 110 ‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥‥(C00 C00 CC0)  Output = (BWϴ PMY CGM)  🖤🤍🧡   💜🩷💛   🩵💚🩷  This would layer the three QR codes data in a readable way... and would look way prettier than the plain B+W codes. colour would be nice, but i feel it would make them a lot harder to read, since it would be so dependent on ambient lighting conditions. you would need a small number of colours that are maximally readable. my first thought would be primary colours: cyan, magenta, and yellow, since they will be maximal of one colour and will add 3 new dimensions to each bit, now each square instead of holding 1 bit could hold 1 nibble or half a byte (cyan, magenta, yellow, black) but if you start combining them( as you would have to do in that example) it makes basically every colour, also black would be redundant here since black is just cyan + magenta + yellow so you can't really get a whole nibble, and if you combine them you go back to lighting problems, if you don't then you have only 4 possibilities for 1 square which means you went from 1 bit to....2 bits. not really worth it.
10 years ago I had a project at a company. Management required the project only be done in excel with VBA. It was our entire data collection process for people working in the field.  Each day everyone working in the field was required to send an update to their managers but this was rarely done and even when it was it often had mistakes.  I added a QR code that had a short breakdown of all work done that day and had the field guys scan it with their phone and send it as a text to their respective managers.  Not the best way to do any of this but it was what I had and immediately we started getting uniform updates from nearly all jobs. It was 4% to 95% overnight.  That excel sheet has withstood a decade and several attempts to replace it. I now only hear about this from people that still work there but it is still in use.  That is a lot of miles out of a badly ported QR generator.

You miss something importand. There was a mechanical telegraph before Morse. Morse-code could exist first after inventing electricity. Barcode was possibe first after inventing Laser. QR Code was first possible after inventing faster Computer. So the technic had to change from 1-bit (electricity) to 8-bit (first Computer), to modern technic. PS: Also SOS was not the first SOS. The first Morse-code was CQD. But here the same. The earlier distress signals are flags and rockets. CQD or SOS was first possible after inventing long range radio. It's a 8 system, so 1 kbyte is NOT 1 KILObyte (because 1 Kilo is 10system-1000), ist 8 system 1024Byte, shown the "k" in kByte is small written, and not a big "K" for Kilo (I hope I didn't mixed up the zeros, I haven't done that for 20 years).

The explanation of redundancy, self correction and R-S codes is awesome.  More facts:  a) if you damage squares in corners, code will easily become unreadable (invisible for the QR reader). No redundancy here. b) uniform areas do not decrease readability as stated in video. Timing strips define the size of the dot and version. Version defines number of dots (width, height). Then the whole code is cut into individual dots and each dot (several grayscale pixels) is then assigned a color (black/white). No problem with uniform areas, see the source code. c) There are several alignment dots in the QR, not only one, depends on QR size. They can be used to detect and unwarp warped parts of the large barcodes by advanced QR readers (not fix the deformation caused by position of the camera as stated in video). It can improve readabililty of large barcodes. You can easily see many alignment squares in large QR codes.
Patients can carry a usb stick with the data in cases of emergency, much more capacity than with any visual data storages. Our devices (phones, whatever equipment doctors use) should work with these storages, it pains me that phone manufactures don't do this.   QR codes seem outdated to me—there's a more efficient way to use space. In essence, the process looks like this: [data] <encoder [encoder weights]> compressed visual representation <decoder [encoder weights]> data. If we could create an encoder that minimizes its size (i.e., number of weights) while still ensuring 100% accurate data recovery despite errors, it would outperform current methods. A neural autoencoder comes to mind—trained to compress specific data types and handle varied error patterns (e.g., garbled chunks, not just bit flips). By shrinking the encoder until it fails, we'd find the most efficient algorithm, likely the theoretical best.

It had only a small storage capacity and you had to be connected to the Internet to use it.  I believe that it simply encoded a serial number which was transmitted to a Microsoft server which then looked up the required data and returned them to the user.  They were free to read, but I think a fee had to be paid to Microsoft to create and use them.  Unsurprisingly, they weren’t widely used, and were short lived.  The only place I ever saw them used was on posters at some stations served by Northern Trains here in the UK; Bradford Forster Square and Ilkley Stations both had them for example, but not all stations served by the company did.  Could never see the point in them; QR codes could do more, could be used on material printed in only one colour, and were free to use.

Oh, one of the most successful (IMO) uses of QR codes for consumer purposes is in the Animal Crossing games. Custom patterns in Animal Crossing: New Leaf could be stored as up to 4 QR codes, so you could send them to other players without needing to explain the steps they'd have to replicate to create the design. Although as designed they included name and town information as encryption, third party sites allowed you to create your own from photos or easily recolor an existing design. Compare that to Animal Crossing: New Horizons, which only uses QR codes for importing New Leaf designs and has a massive color palette, so copying complex images is far more labor intensive. As a result, many players just designed custom patterns on those same third party websites and imported them as if from New Leaf, or else communicated the colors using idiosyncratic methods like codes and color lookup tables. I would've gone hog wild for NH to have QR codes over all that.
I was early in my marketing career as a graphic designer when QR codes started becoming known and I both loved and HATED them. Sooooo many people wanted to use them to look tech forward but would often print them at unusable sizes. I also told most of my clients “until Apple makes reading QR codes a feature built into iOS they won’t reach mass adoption” Having to download an app and then those apps often sucked, and the people making QR codes would do a bad job of getting it the right size, AND use it for simple URLs that were easier to type in, all made them not make sense for quite a few years.  What I loved about them at the time was the potential I saw they had if used in ways that actually made sense! More complex URLs, vCard info, trackbility to analytics from printed marketing materials, etc. Essentially what we see now. It took a while, decisions from top tech companies, and a global pandemic, but QR codes are definitley just a normal part of life now.

your history on morse code is lacking and flawed in one tiny bit.. 1: the morse code that you listed was not the morse code that samuel morse designed. what we normally use today is the internationally standardized variant of morse code. the video appears like we adopted Mr. Morse's version. we did not. 2: the first morse code did not sound like it does today. the dash was two clicks and the dot was two clicks. there was just a time difference between the two clicks per dot and per dash. the tone version didn't come along until later. 3: in the video, SOS was played with a space of letter spaces between them. this is incorrect. SOS shud be sent as: ...---... and not ...  ---  ... There shud only be the shortest space between the letters of SOS, which is the delay between dots and dashes (one time unit, which is the time unit of a dot), not a space of 3 time units which is the space delay between letters. in other words, SOS shud be ran together, not sent as three separate letters.

All I have to do then is once I've booted the computer is open a command prompt, scan the QR and hey presto, the job is done and I have the drive set up ready to go. Yep, they're really useful. I also use barcodes quite a bit as well. I'm a radio ham and quite often I have to program radios. I have created a book with every frequency across several bands with its barcode so when I'm programming a radio I just scan the frequency(s) I need. Saves a lot of typing and drastically reduces errors.
LOL @ 12:10.  "a square pattern on a white background. The information encoded may be text, a URL, or other data. Created by Toyota subsidiary Denso Wave in 1994, the QR code is one of the most popular types of two-dimensional barcodes. The QR code was designed to allow its contents to be decoded at high speed. The technology has seen frequent use in Japan and South Korea; the United Kingdom is the seventh-largest national consumer of QR codes. Although initially used for tracking parts in vehicle manufacturing, QR codes now are used in a much broader context, including both commercial tracking applications and convenience-oriented applications aimed at mobile phone users (termed mobile tagging). QR codes may be used to display text to the user, to add a vCard contact to the user's device, to open a Uniform Resource Identifier (URI), or to compose an e-mail or text message. Users can generate and print their own QR codes for others to scan and use by visiting one of several paid and free QR code generating sites or apps."

This was a modern day equivalent of magical symbols which when one reads some magic stuff happens. It was fascinating to see it work, it still manages to surprise me every time even after knowing how it works. Truly whimsical I must say. QR codes are a huge security risk:  Invisible URLs: Scanning a QR code automatically opens a URL or app, but the user can't see where it leads until after scanning. Malicious codes can link to harmful websites or download malicious files without the user’s knowledge.  Phishing: A QR code can direct users to a phishing website that looks legitimate but is designed to steal personal information, such as login credentials or financial details.  Malware Distribution: QR codes can trigger automatic downloads of malware or viruses to a user's device, especially on devices with lax security settings.  Payment Fraud: QR codes used in payment systems can be replaced or tampered with, redirecting payments to fraudulent accounts.  Location Spoofing: Malicious QR codes can link to a location-based service and send false location information, causing security or privacy issues.  Lack of Human Verification: Unlike a traditional URL, a QR code doesn’t offer a clear indication of its destination, making it easier for attackers to deceive users.

I could scan a QR code when so much of it was missing - it was almost scary. And I don’t think I’ve ever had a QR code resolve to something inaccurate - either it errors, or it’s 100% right. It’s so interesting to see grade school math be so ubiquitous and important in modern day computing.
12:10 Version 40 QR Code can contain up to 4296 chars. A QR code (abbreviated from Quick Response code) is a type of matrix barcode (or two-dimensional code) that is designed to be read by smartphones. The code consists of black modules arranged in a square pattern on a white background. The information encoded may be text, a URL, or other data. Toward the end, Mashiro Hara says that he would like to make use of these codes in disaster response. In the early 2000's I was working on my institution's disaster response team and I tried to get them to adopt a barcode bracelet system for use by first responders who could use one sheet with the same barcode and multiple bracelets to attach to family members rescued from a scene who would need treatment at different hospitals. The bracelet info could contain other things, like which FEMA incident, the local responder entity, and a blank space for written info. For example, sending a child to a pediatric hospital, maybe an adult to a Level I Trauma Center, etc. Each bracelet being left on until the family was, hopefully, reunited. And, during hospitalization, each member of the family could be tracked by other members. This method allowed receiving hospitals to also use their own bracelets. I think the idea of encoding medical information in QR format is a great idea. My heartfelt thanks to Mashiro Hara for his perseverance and to you, the Veritasium team, for posting this video.

Were these subtitles auto-generated or produced by a human translator? I see errors like this in single language auto-generated subtitles or speech to text all the time. Let alone Machine Speech to Text followed by Machine Translation, where the potential for error is grossly amplified,  and does not lend the same possibility for 'Error Correction' as the QR Code ;-)  In Japanese, the words Tōkyō or Toukyou (two plain consonant sounds with double length vowel sounds or dashes) and Tokkyo (a plain consonant followed by a short vowel sounds or Dot, with a Glottal Stop stoccato consonant k in the middle followed by another short vowel sounds or another Dot) sound completely different. People need to be vary careful with auto-generated subtitles and translations, as errors can creep in, go undetected, with potential serious ramifications...
The argument that qr codes make it easier to phish people was always dead in the water. It's literally just a way to write some text, a way to input text on your device by camera instead of by eye and manual typing. I actually even open a qr generator site on my pc when I want to copy-paste some non-trivial text to my phone. The actual meaning of that text is up to you to evaluate after seeing it and before acting on it. If you fell for a phony qr link, you'd fall for the same phony url if it was written out and you had to type it over by hand. You'd have to scan it first instead of seeing right off the bat what the link is, but there's no practical difference other than the time lost scanning it. The only real risk is qr scanners acting (like opening links) on a scanned qr without showing you the content first, which is another among hundreds of security mistakes a dev can make (and a user too should realize after the first time that they don't know which links they're opening before they open), but it's not qr's fault. I think app stores have started cracking down on it too, just for good measure.
I'm the opposite of you. I always hated barcodes, but I love QR codes. Obviously both are really languages for machines, though technically speaking a human can learn to read them. In fact, because QR codes actually encode text I think they are a bit more human-friendly than barcodes were. All you have to learn is 1) how to read ASCII-encoded letters, which is no more complex than learning to read another alphabet and 2) where to find them on the QR code. You don't need to worry about the redundancy encoding, because your brain's innate estimation ability should be enough to read a damaged code. I was that kid who learned to read barcodes, but the usefulness of that skill beyond parlor tricks was always limited. Reading a barcode just gave you a (usually) meaningless string of twelve numbers. You then had to look up whether the numbers meant anything. Learning to read a QR code might be slightly more complex but once you have learned you can actually read meaningful text off of the code.  However, that's not the reason why I like QR codes and disliked barcodes. I like QR codes because they are pretty😂.

So not a programmer here, but had this crazy idea that just might be plausible in the right overly intelligent hands.... With the largest data containing version of a QR code itself being valuable if holding the entirety of the Snake program, could you use QR codes AS a programming language? Could you create a series of QR codes that contain programming language and instruction on how to align the total group of QR codes into an arrangement that itself becomes a larger QR code, made up of cells that are either black or white based on whether there are more black or white bits total in that code, using the various masking methods to ensure it is read in the way that you need it to appear to give the correct bit encoding required, so that the mega QR code itself is just a series of QR code patterns and masking methods for each so you can read the entire sequence different ways hundreds of times over out of the one overarching QR code... Like compressing your entire code into a zip file which can be decompressed and ran just by brute forcing a QR reader program at it over and over until you complete the instructions?
just so everyone knows, adding color would increase the amount of saveable data exponentially. white is 0, black is 1, red is 2. this is trinary, and is far more efficient than binary. adding just the three base colors (RGB), would give us 5 different colors to use, which gives every single pixel the ability to store 5 different pieces of information. for each pixel you add, this is multiplied by 5. 5, 25, 125, 625, 3125, 15625, 78125, 390625 options, and we have only just reached the equivalent of one byte (which has 256). just like this, we can halve the number of digits we use and still have 2.4 times as many things we can uniquely identify in that space. now add the secondary colors, bringing you to 8 options. make a light and dark version of each color, 14 options. use saturation to double them yet again, 26. add gray to get to 27, and we are still able to tell each one apart with ease. now we can store 2.8 bytes worth of information in TWO digits! thats 282,429,536,481 unique combinations of information from a single byte equivalent. you could store a ridiculous amount of information in the same amount of space.
Of course you talk about none of the real fundamental issues with QR codes which is security and trust. There is no built-in trust mechanism that I as a human can validate what is contained in the QR code is the information that it's intended to be. So basically for any link out to a third-party service or a link for a payment application it's the credit card skimmer problem but about 100,000 times easier to pull off.   So the only legitimate use for QR codes as they are presented today is when you have a high degree of trust for what it is. A ticket stub you bought the ticket it prints a QR code they're scan the ticket you have a high degree of trust that it's simply an identification for a ticket like a barcode. But stores with sell checkout already battle with barcode issues of people swiping little barcodes on high priced items with lower priced items and stealing. So again it's a very limited trust situation you cannot generally trust these things out in the wild you can only trust them in very specific instances and until that problem is solved QR codes are trash and should remain in the trash cuz they are a massive security risk.

[boring story: please skip this] I used to have a small landscaping business before the COVID thing in USA, and I had a QR code on my trucks (with logo and brand name and website of course, and phone number.). My business spread mostly by word of mouth by my customers, and by my truck being well known in the neighborhood. Back to the QR: I just checked my website stats maybe monthly or so, my website logged only like several QR code scans a year. And I didn't see any new customers because of the QR, but I had a few already customers pretty much say 'oh wow, it brought up this'.... [/end of boring story]
I have a guilty pleasure: BuzzFeed. They had a feature from about 15 or 20 years ago showing the top ten failed technologies. Number one (and it will shock you) was the QR code. Because you needed a special app to read it. Now, every camera on every phone will read QRs.   We were traveling south on St Paul Street in Baltimore. Across the intersection with 25th St was a 10 storey building with a 2 storey tall QR. my wife gestured, “What the hell are those things?” I pulled out my phone, scanned it and showed her the results, “Looks like a nice place to live. A little pricey but they have a pool and a gym.”  I work in an Apple Store. Seniors are often reluctant to adopt new technology. Not so QRs. I had a 92 year old customer pull out his phone and scan a QR like it was nothing.   When we moved, I made inventory lists of every box we packed. I got a roll of QR code stickers and put one on each box. Using an app, I link each box’s QR with each inventory list. When we got to our new home, we simply scanned the QR and we knew what was in each box. I found our towels, bed linens, and my computer with no problem.   I love this failed technology.
"QR codes are a language for machines, and I am a human"  That sentence reveals THE reason that QR codes have become so ubiquitous, and increasingly important. Yes, we are humans. However, we humans are also becoming more and more integrated into machines. The QR code is one small step along that path. The QR code is so useful because almost all of us are now tethered to a device that makes using that code incredibly easy. In other words, we are slowly marching toward becoming a "Borg" species, fully integrated into our technology.   I first realized this a couple of decades ago when I noticed how so many people were drawn to the idea of constantly sharing their daily lives through social media. It brought to mind a line from Star Trek, where a Borg character speaks about always having the thoughts of the others in their mind. I knew then that we were truly headed down the path to the eventual result. It is a result that the show portrayed very negatively, but as a species we are willingly walking that path.   AI will probably not exterminate us. More likely we will willingly (albeit gradually) merge with it and allow ourselves to be "assimilated".
As a kid in the 90's I remember stumbling on an end-times conspiracy website that was trying to claim that the 3 registration marks in a UPC were 6's... so every UPC had a '666' and was evil and for tracking.  But I noticed that every example they gave didn't have a '6' in it... so for fun and curiosity I went in the kitchen and pulled a bunch of cans off the shelf and reverse engineered UPC, and it was so cool to see the actual numbers and the reversing pattern.  I didn't realize it was set up like a MAC address where there is a vendor and a product half, but the message itself was plain as day.  But I was relieved to find that the registration marks are not 6's, and the devil wasn't involved in tracking every product ever bought (it's just corporate America... way better lol).  When I started seeing more QR codes in my area a few years ago I was hopeful to do something similar and figure it out myself... but this video really explains why that was impossible.  The way that the data is structured, and multiple formats that it could be structured in would make it practically impossible to reverse-engineer with the few QR codes that I have available to me in daily life.
Morse code is an example of how we can get stuck with a sub-par standard due to rushed adoption. The way it was originally developed, it was effectively a _ternary_ code, because without a third (implicit) symbol, the "space", there would be no difference between "EEE" and "S", or between "NA" and "X". And because that space is also the separator between characters (and the separator between words), the message can easily become garbled due to bad technique on the sender's part (ex., making the spaces between characters too short, or making the spaces within characters too long).  With accurate timing, it _can_ be translated directly into a digital signal (instead of "dots" and "dashes", you have just a sequence of ones and zeros where dot/dit=1, dash/dah=111, space between them (within the same letter)=0, space between letters=000 and space between words=0000000), but with just a little bit more work (and some input from mathematicians), a Huffman-style table of optimised binary encodings could have been defined right from the start, which would have been a lot more resilient to user error, a lot easier to automate, and would probably have helped launch the digital revolution several decades sooner.
Since you put a lot of work into this video, I don't want to be critical, but I do have a complaint. Only a QR code programmer would care about error detection and correction, but that was about a third of the video (felt like). Boring waste of time. But the one thing everyone cares about is encoding. And you fell woefully short there. Your creation was to put your website's URL in a QR code. That extremely tiny bit of info took up half the grid. But you tell us that the latest QR code version can store an almost infinite number of combinations. So what? That is not the same thing as "a lot of information" because random combinations are the opposite of information. If the encoding format is ASCII, even the latest incarnation of the QR code will hold a trivial amount of information. Your 10xE52 comment made it sound like you could put Wikipedia on a QR code, when in reality, you can't even put a long paragraph of text on a QR code if your encoding was in ASCII. Bottom line: You really never told me how a lot of data could be stored on a code. You didn't address images, or anything else. So, all I learned is what those blocks in the corners mean and how the basic layout flows, using 0s and 1s. I still don't know squat.
The video skipped the punch card checkout. Obviously, only very few (large) stores had these. Instead of the usual price tags (which were printed and stuck to the product by a "tag pistol"), they attached tiny punch cards to each product.  At the checkout, the punch cards were teared off, collected on a special holder and fed into the cash register, which would then add everything up and print the bill. No funny business of getting all the stuff out of your shopping cart to pile it onto a counter or a belt, an employee would come the cart, collect the punch cards and hand them over to the cashier. Of course you needed to stack stuff in your cart strategically to allow access to the tear-off punch cards. The carts were much wider than the ones we have today, so that was not really an issue. But they needed more space, so this system was used only in huge shops. And more spacious checkouts, so the employees could circle around your cart, but without belts (and the cashiers sitting in boxes at the wall, totally out of the way) this wasn't as bad as it sounds at first. But still.  Nowadays, handheld barcode scanners allow for checkouts where you can keep the stuff on your cart. Some large hardware stores use this, also at self-checkouts.
Version 40 QR Code can contain up to 4296 chars. A QR code (abbreviated from Quick Response code) is a type of matrix barcode (or two-dimensional code) that is designed to be read by smartphones. The code consists of black modules arranged in a square pattern on a white background. The information encoded may be text, a URL, or other data. Created by Toyota subsidiary Denso Wave in 1994, the QR code is one of the most popular types of two-dimensional barcodes. The QR code was designed to allow its contents to be decoded at high speed. The technology has seen frequent use in Japan and South Korea; the United Kingdom is the seventh-largest national consumer of QR codes. Although initially used for tracking parts in vehicle manufacturing, QR codes now are used in a much broader context, including both commercial tracking applications and convenience-oriented applications aimed at mobile phone users (termed mobile tagging). QR codes may be used to display text to the user, to add a vCard contact to the user's device, to open a Uniform Resource Identifier (URI), or to compose an e-mail or text message. Users can generate and print their own QR codes for others to scan and use by visiting one of several paid and free QR code generating sites or apps.

A QR code (abbreviated from Quick Response code) is a type of matrix barcode (or two-dimensional code) that is designed to be read by smartphones. The code consists of black modules arranged in a square pattern on a white background. The information encoded may be text, a URL, or other data. Created by Toyota subsidiary Denso Wave in 1994, the QR code is one of the most popular types of two-dimensional barcodes. The QR code was designed to allow its contents to be decoded at high speed. The technology has seen frequent use in Japan and South Korea; the United Kingdom is the seventh-largest national consumer of QR codes. Although initially used for tracking parts in vehicle manufacturing, QR codes now are used in a much broader context, including both commercial tracking applications and convenience-oriented applications aimed at mobile phone users (termed mobile tagging). QR codes may be used to display text to the user, to add a vCard contact to the user's device, to open a Uniform Resource Identifier (URI), or to compose an e-mail or text message. Users can generate and print their own QR codes for others to scan and use by visiting one of several paid and free QR code generating sites or apps.

This image illustrates the similarities between the Sinosphere languages, which are primarily influenced by Chinese. The languages compared are Chinese, Vietnamese, Korean, and Japanese. The connections and similarities shown in the diagram are:  1. Vietnamese and Chinese:  Both are tonal languages with similar analytical grammar.  Roughly 60% of Vietnamese vocabulary comes from Chinese.    2. Chinese and Korean:  About 60% of Korean vocabulary is derived from Chinese.  Both languages use classifiers for counting different objects (e.g., animals, books, cups).    3. Chinese and Japanese:  About 60% of Japanese vocabulary comes from Chinese.  Both languages use Kanji/Hanzi (Chinese characters).    4. Korean and Japanese:  Both languages have agglutinative grammar and follow the subject-object-verb (SOV) structure.  They both have formality levels, and Korean has some Japanese loanwords.    5. Korean and Vietnamese:  Both languages share a system of formality levels.    6. All Four Languages:  All four use classifiers for counting different types of objects, which is a feature commonly found in East Asian languages.     In summary, the languages share significant structural, lexical, and grammatical similarities due to their shared historical connections with Chinese influence.
12:10 qr code that literally says:Version 40 QR Code can contain up to 4296 chars. A QR code (abbreviated from Quick Response code) is a type of matrix barcode (or two-dimensional code) that is designed to be read by smartphones. The code consists of black modules arranged in a square pattern on a white background. The information encoded may be text, a URL, or other data. Created by Toyota subsidiary Denso Wave in 1994, the QR code is one of the most popular types of two-dimensional barcodes. The QR code was designed to allow its contents to be decoded at high speed. The technology has seen frequent use in Japan and South Korea; the United Kingdom is the seventh-largest national consumer of QR codes. Although initially used for tracking parts in vehicle manufacturing, QR codes now are used in a much broader context, including both commercial tracking applications and convenience-oriented applications aimed at mobile phone users (termed mobile tagging). QR codes may be used to display text to the user, to add a vCard contact to the user's device, to open a Uniform Resource Identifier (URI), or to compose an e-mail or text message. Users can generate and print their own QR codes for others to scan and use by visiting one of several paid and free QR code generating sites or apps.

The extremely obvious way to make QR codes safe, would be to make the reader app decode the thing and display the resulting URL in the clear for the user to examine, and only proceed to visit the site if the user takes an additional confirming action.  But cellphone makers aren't eager to do that, because it would defeat the whole point of QR codes as far as advertisers are concerned.  No one official will ever admit this, but the entire purpose of using QR codes is to obscure the link they're presenting you with, in hopes that you'll be more likely to follow it if you don't know where it goes.  OCR technology is good enough that they could just print the URL and let you scan it and go to it, but you wouldn't bother, and that's why QR codes are used instead.  I'm not saying they don't have any other applications, because they do.  But those other applications are the exception rather than the rule.  More than 95% of the time, if they just gave you the URL, you would immediately know you have no interest in it, and that's why they're giving you the QR code, because they're hoping you can't resist the allure of the unknown.  If cellphone makers neutered the deceptive power of QR codes by making the bundled decoder app show you the URL rather than just loading the page, the popularity of QR codes would nosedive.
