For anyone wondering what the version 40 QR code shows it says this; Version 40 QR Code can contain up to 4296 chars. A QR code (abbreviated from Quick Response code) is a type of matrix barcode (or two-dimensional code) that is designed to be read by smartphones. The code consists of black modules arranged in a square pattern on a white background. The information encoded may be text, a URL, or other data. Created by Toyota subsidiary Denso Wave in 1994, the QR code is one of the most popular types of two-dimensional barcodes. The QR code was designed to allow its contents to be decoded at high speed. The technology has seen frequent use in Japan and South Korea; the United Kingdom is the seventh-largest national consumer of QR codes. Although initially used for tracking parts in vehicle manufacturing, QR codes now are used in a much broader context, including both commercial tracking applications and convenience-oriented applications aimed at mobile phone users (termed mobile tagging). QR codes may be used to display text to the user, to add a vCard contact to the user's device, to open a Uniform Resource Identifier (URI), or to compose an e-mail or text message. Users can generate and print their own QR codes for others to scan and use by visiting one of several paid and free QR code generating sites or apps.
Version 1: "I'm the OG" Version 2: "I'm a bog-standard QR Code" Verson 40: :Version 40 QR Code can contain up to 4296 chars. A QR code (abbreviated from Quick Response code) is a type of matrix barcode (or two-dimensional code) that is designed to be read by smartphones. The code consists of black modules arranged in a square pattern on a white background. The information encoded may be text, a URL, or other data. Created by Toyota subsidiary Denso Wave in 1994, the QR code is one of the most popular types of two-dimensional barcodes. The QR code was designed to allow its contents to be decoded at high speed. The technology has seen frequent use in Japan and South Korea; the United Kingdom is the seventh-largest national consumer of QR codes. Although initially used for tracking parts in vehicle manufacturing, QR codes now are used in a much broader context, including both commercial tracking applications and convenience-oriented applications aimed at mobile phone users (termed mobile tagging). QR codes may be used to display text to the user, to add a vCard contact to the user's device, to open a Uniform Resource Identifier (URI), or to compose an e-mail or text message. Users can generate and print their own QR codes for others to scan and use by visiting one of several paid and free QR code generating sites or apps."

There is a whole chapter in my Topology textbook (actually two of them) that go over the mathematics behind how it works. The ONLY reason I remember this is because those questions were on the GRE. Graduate level classes and test questions you would literally have to have already graduated to take the class for (It wouldn't be covered by an intro to Topology class, I doub't you'd even see it until the 3rd one). QR codes use the same math. It's actually kind of neat because it's not based on absolutes. This math came out in the 80's and was essential for the operation of bar codes. A QR code is just a fancy bar code so the math still applies.  Edit: This doesn't include the actual information conveyed, just how to interface the scanning technology with the information requested (the fuzziness). The less "fuzz" you have, the smaller the code can be. (this is actually a topology concept referring to open and closed spaces, so point set topology shows up here). The neat part is despite the fuzziness of the scanner, the code being scanned, and the reflection not being a direct line to line reflection (it's more of a cone), the system works because of how the topology of it is designed.  So his talk about it is sadly disappointing to me because it leaves out the mathematics that actually power it (the bar codes and number storage is a number theory question)

The code consists of black modules arranged in a square pattern on a white background. The information encoded may be text, a URL, or other data. Users can generate and print their own QR codes for others to scan and use by visiting one of several paid and free QR code generating sites or apps.

1. UPC are actually 13 digit codes... Just because the first digit in US and Canada is a 0 which is usually omitted by those countries. It still has value to the form of construction of the barcode (the first digit determines the sequence of the up to 3 variants a number can be displayed) 2. I still very much dislike QR-codes. A Barcode only needs a µm thick line to scan successfully that's less then 1% of the total surface of the barcode. Yet the QR-code needs at least 70% of the surface of the code to be readable. I Can already imagine the wrinkled pack of cookies not scanning, because the packaging has nowhere a stable flat surface to read the code on.  They are OK, the surface can be guaranteed flat. But a nightmare for anything non-flat. even with 75 years of experience you still have distributors that  print unreadable barcodes. The number of Unreadable QR-codes will just increase.  Don't get me wrong in the situations where flat surfaces can work QR's are very sufficient and superior. Yet there are still way too many circumstances a flat surface can't be guaranteed, and due to how QR is now pushed over UPC by those that hand out the first SEVEN  digit range of barcodes (GS1). I fear we will roll back in efficiency for at least a decade of time, because the nimrods pushing this have way too little practicle "field" experience how these scannable codes work in real life scenarios.
Back in early 2010s I wrote a small JavaScript library for encoding QR code (lifthrasiir/qr.js in GitHub), which remains modestly popular even today. This video covers many of them, but there are also other small details that ultimately contribute to the decoding process. It really helps to look at those details to appreciate the design of QR code, at least I felt so back then.  13:36 It should be noted that even though different sizes are termed "versions", they are not really extensible. Indeed the earlier version of QR code (AIM ITS 97-001) is called "Model 1" while the current version is called "Model 2", and the standard notes that these two models are difficult, while not impossible, to tell apart from each other by unsuspecting decoders. The Model 2 QR code also includes four additional smallers versions M1 through M4, which are called Micro QR.  32:31 I'm not sure whether Mashiro Hara is aware of any of them, but there exist several 2D barcode symbologies that are designed to be either higher capacity or using multiple colors (e.g. HCC2D). It is however very unclear how much additional capacity we can get from them, because a larger number of pixels requires a higher resolution in the current pixel-based scheme and the largest QR code version (177 x 177) is already not far from that limitation. Anything larger than 10 KB will probably need a very different approach; one proposal used multiple different QR codes in time, for example.

QR code have so many uses!!! I have them set up in my house for guest to scan an autoconnect them to the internet Wi-Fi. I can also cut entire messages in one, so starting all my stuff in my garage and storage bins put them on the outside of every box and scan it to see everything that's inside. And then also to organize mine 3D printer filament rack, I have all the various colors and filaments materials all separated out the scan and load up. And then I also have All of my medical items with medicines the amount I take, and any other medical conditions and stuff in a QR code that's printed off and laminated to the size of a credit card that sits next to my medical card. And so anybody can scan it if need be and get all my medical information there. And there's so many more good uses for them! I have a sheet and Excel, where the data I put into it anytime it changes or whatever the formula it automatically generates a full QR code at that time. I have it looked up to my label printer so whenever I am categorizing stuff I just enter the data into this spreadsheet, and then push print. It automatically generates the QR code and puts it right in the perfect spot on the label. There is actually so many uses and so much data that can be stored into a single QR code. Multiple people have even made computer programs where all you do is you scan the QR code and that's the entire program. However, this can also be done as a virus and innocent person comes up and scans it and poof they're infected.

A quick note about barcodes from someone who used to work with them a lot in a software setting. The bar codes you're specifically talking about are UPC-A codes, which consist of 12 digits. There are several other barcode formats as well, extending into even alphanumeric barcodes (which began in 1981 thanks to the development of Code-128), which means there are essentially infinite bar codes. Another trick is that, for example, 00000001 is different from 0001 is different from 01. It's reading the numbers as a string rather than an integer, which allows for the other UPC variations such as UPC-E, or other shorter barcodes you might find on produce.  You also got it slightly incorrect on how the scanner knows the beginning and end of the barcode. This is done by a Start symbol and a Stop symbol. The check symbol isn't the last symbol in the bar code. A lot of software can even be configured to have specific start/stop symbols.  The main limitation of bar codes is their size and, as you mentioned, the destructibility. And while there's technically an infinite number of bar codes that could be made thanks to Code-128, there was a theoretical limit (and I think a couple hard coded limits) to the length of a bar code. It also required a special tool, or special software (while it's now ubiquitous for smart phones to scan QR codes, you would still need special software or even a special peripheral to properly scan a bar code). QR codes do not (well, they do, but it's ubiquitous to any smart phone now) 2D barcodes (QR codes and others) are also much better at encoding information.

One of the simplest ways to enhance the data capacity of QR codes is by incorporating multiple colors—strategically chosen, of course. If implemented correctly, the addition of colors can significantly increase the amount of information each QR code can hold. It stands to reason that this concept has been explored previously; there are likely research papers or commercial products that already utilize colored QR codes.  Moreover, I believe every QR code that carry a certain amount of data that could be used nefariously or to point to a URL that can be to a malware should include a certificate that points to the issuer as a method to mitigate security risks and combat fraud and if the certificate isn't valid then the QR code must be rejected and the end-user notified about the problem in order for them to report it or be more cautious. This would add a layer of trust and accountability to the usage of QR codes, ensuring that users can verify the source of the information before acting on it.  Backward compatibility is another essential aspect to consider for any new QR code designs. While the vast computing power available in modern devices should largely alleviate concerns regarding compatibility, it remains crucial to ensure that new QR codes can be easily read by older scanning technology. This can likely be addressed through software solutions and intelligent algorithms that can interpret the various new formats.  I must admit that I never anticipated the aesthetic potential of QR codes until viewing this video. While I’ve always appreciated the concept, I was unaware of the intricate mathematics involved in error-checking and correction that underpin their functionality. This complexity not only enhances their reliability but also opens up exciting avenues for design innovation.


As some others noted, the video mispoke about ASCII values (and 8 bit binary values in general) at 11:06. I am offering some explanation for those who would like to undertand why he is mistaken.  TLDR; It is true that ASCII encoding uses 8 binary bits to represent 256 (2^8) >>values<<. However the >>range<< of those values is from 0 to 255 ... not 1 to 256 as stated in the video.  Long Explanation: The largest value represented with 8 bits in binary is 11111111  The decimal equivalent is 2^7*1 + 2^6*1 + 2^5*1 + 2^4*1 + 2^3*1 + 2^2*1 + 2^1*1 2^0*1 = 128 + 64 + 32 + 16 + 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 255. To represent 256 in binary you would need 9 bits: 100000000 = 2^8^1  2^7*0 + 2^6*0 + 2^5*0 + 2^4*0 + 2^3*0 + 2^2*0 + 2^1*0 + 2^0*0 = 256 Converting his example of the lower case letter 'w' as 8-bit binary 01110111 would be 2^7*0 + 2^6*1 + 2^5*1 + 2^4*1 + 2^3*0 + 2^2*1 + 2^1*1 + 2^0*1 = 0 + 64 + 32 + 16 + 0 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 119  Saying you can represent values from 1 to 256 with 8-bit binary is like saying you can represent the values from 1 to 100 with 2-digit decimal numbering. There are 100 (2^10) >>values<< but the >>range<< is 0 to 99.   Decimal (or base 10 numbering ... ten values per digit from 0-9) works the same as binary (or base 2 numbering ... two value sper digit from 0-1). Just like with binary, each digit has a maximum value that it can represent. So 99 decimal = 10^1*9 + 10^0*9 = 90 + 9 = 99. We don't think about it that way with decimal numbering because we use them so often, but the concept is the same.  If you want to go futher down the rabbit hole you can learn about hexidecimal (base 16 numbering) with sixteen values per digit represented by 0-9 and A-F which is a great shorthand representation of binary (4 binary bits per heximdeimcal digit). Early in Computer Science there was also Octal (base 8 numbering). Outside of modern Computer Science you can also find Sexagecimal (base 60 numbering) used by ancient civilizations that help simplify many astromical calculations.  I hope that helps someone unerstand better!
@veritasium As someone who has written software to create 2D barcodes, I found this deep dive into the history of them to be really interesting and I definitely feel your pain crafting one by hand. But, I think you missed a trick in your question about why QR codes are so successful. In my opinion, QR codes are so successful at capturing the public's imagination because they are providing 2 levels of communication: -To a computer they communicate whatever information is encoded in them. -To the human observer, they say "hey, you can scan me" The distinctive appearance makes them a recognizable cypher to curious humans.  By contrast, datamatrix codes are far more populous and entirely ignored by humans. You are surrounded by them right now and may not even know it. Go look at the bottles and tubes of stuff under your kitchen sink or in your bathroom. If you look closely, you will probably find at least 5 datamatrix codes that you never noticed before. I'm looking at a bottle of Aveeno moisturizer right now and there is a datamatrix code on the front of it -- not on the back with the UPC; Johnson&Johnson is so sure that this datamatrix code is invisible to consumers that they put it right on the front with all of their high-profile branding. And that is just the visible DM codes around you. There are magnitudes more that are actually invisible; because they are hidden inside on circuit boards, ICs, sub-assemblies; inside the dust jackets of books; folded over on cardboard that you don't see (along with the color swatches). Datamatrix codes are used ubiquitously in factory information systems because of their compact size and, since lighting and other conditions can be controlled in a factory, they don't have to worry about all of the downsides that QR is trying to solve.  So, I just wanted to mention this -- both types of 2D code are communicating with humans just like they communicate with machines, but they send different messages. QR codes say, "look at me and scan me for more information!" Datamatrix codes say, "ignore me, I'm invisible and pointless to you."
"I used to hate QR codes. But they're actually genius"? More like "I used to hate QR codes. And I was right all along." QR codes are a prime example of the human race settling for poorly designed standards, and it's a tragedy that we keep accepting mediocrity and inefficiency in so many areas of our lives. Let's start with the obvious: QR codes are a waste of space and potential. They use predefined information types instead of just encoding raw data, limiting flexibility and efficiency. The measly four error correction levels are a joke, and the gigantic alignment pixels eat up valuable real estate. Fixed code sizes? Please. We should be able to scale the code based on the data we're encoding, not be limited by arbitrary restrictions. But it gets worse. QR codes apply error correction to the user data, but not to the encoded data as a whole. This means that if the special pixels aren't read correctly, the entire QR code becomes useless. It's like building a house with a strong foundation but forgetting to put a roof on it. And don't even get me started on the encryption issue. In the video, the inventor of QR codes was asked about encryption, and instead of acknowledging that users can encrypt the data themselves before encoding it, he mentioned a different version of QR codes used in Japan that enables encryption. Great, another variant of an already flawed system, leading to even more waste and fragmentation. But QR codes are just the tip of the iceberg. Look at the shortsightedness of IPv4 – we're now struggling to move to IPv6 because we didn't plan ahead. Or the QWERTY keyboard layout, designed to slow down typing on mechanical typewriters, yet still used on digital devices. And let's not forget the imperial measurement system that caused NASA to lose a $125 million Mars orbiter. It's time we demand better from our technology and stop settling for "genius" designs that are anything but. The consequences of our complacency are far-reaching and devastating, and it's time for a revolution in design thinking. Don't let anyone tell you that QR codes are genius – they're a symptom of a much larger problem that we all need to face head-on.
Hmmm. I don't know. Whilst S.O.S. might have originally been used because of its simplicity; I've believed my whole life (as a sailor that learned to navigate on the ocean by the age of seven) that it was always understood as Save Our Souls. "Souls" meaning persons on board, not a religious connotation specifically (although, yes, that would have been the origin of the term). Today, we simply say "P.O.B." to denote persons onboard, but when I was younger the term "Souls" was most common.  Now, when logging on to rescue services to announce departure, we state "# POB", where '#' represents the number of people. But, in days gone by, we would have logged on with "# souls".  In conclusion, it makes perfect sense that people aboard ships and aircraft traditionally were "souls". You'd have to forgive me for thinking that your claim that S.O.S "meant nothing" is wrong.  What could be more succinct in a moment of requiring assistance than "Save Our Souls"? It is literally asking that someone save all aboard.  I can't deny that we could be looking at a chicken and egg situation. Did they use S.O.S. out of simplicity and then we've referred to persons aboard as Souls as a result? Or did we always call them souls and S.O.S. was the perfect way to request assistance?  I would argue that it doesn't matter, in this case, which came first. Because, even if we assume that the distress signal came first and was adapted to "Save Our Souls", the time delay between the two nullifies the situation.  All that to say that I think you're wrong. Unless you want to be pedantic, it has always been understood by mariners as "Save Our Souls". I think that Titanic was the first usage of S.O.S. I think they also used the previous C.Q.D. If you look into the sinking of the Titanic, they almost certainly intended "S.O.S." to mean Save Our Souls. As this was the first time that the distress had ever been used,. isn't that satisfactorily evident that it "meant something"?  Your statement is more suited to C.Q.D. I don't think it "meant anything" other than to be universally recognised as distress. And, perhaps, it is the distress signal that you've confused here in the video.
I have long thought that not using color was a waste of potential as well, and we already have a system in place for the numbers, if we use traditional RGB values (0-255 each for red green blue) this would set the current 256 options per slot ^256^256^256.  This Would require a complete overhaul in qr printers, color unification and stabilization, and readers.  BUT, with that much extra data, we may be close to, if not at, a point where a visual data set could be more data dense than our current memory chips.  And this only grows further if you use tighter color dilineations, or change the classic 256 ascii code into something larger.  For example, if we boosted both the binomial and the color up to 1024 (fairly simple increase) then we would have basically (for napkin math) how many slots your QR code has, lets use a 128x128 grid for easy base 8 math and a good example (16,384)^1,024(binomial)^1,024(red)^1,024(green)^1,024(blue) This would result in numbers so large that … well, lets put it this way, i crashed 4 “extremely large number calculation” sites, got “infinity” from 3 others, and various versions of “error” from about 20 others.  I considered trying my hand at it manually, but realized how many year, books worth of notes, and so forth that would take.  Even at the original much smaller size (Lets go with 32x32[1024], then the 4x256s) It still errors out the same sites (well, the ones that are back up after the crash or errored out)  Considering that one such site was able to get to 10E100,000,000,000,000,000,000,000,000 digits without failing that should tell you how big we are talking.  Like… more than every grain of sand on every beach.  Heck, we could Massively simplify it (10x10 with a 10 per color code (aka something you can have a toddler put together with sports balls on a mat) and you would have 100^10^10^10^10 and even THAT breaks the calculation resources i have on hand.  (And at that value the accuracy of colors needs not be all That accurate)  making it so that, if you could get molecular color and scanning down, you could store more data than everything humanity has ever done on a surface smaller than the tip of a sewing needle, and with redundancy built in.
Being useful doesn't mean they're not still a bad deal.  Your initial dislike of them was warranted.  They're useful due to the prevalence of 'smart phones' which themselves are a bit of a drag on society, people developing dependency rather than thinking and learning to communicate effectively in person.  QR codes may be fascinating technologically speaking, but they're just a small part of the negative side of high technology.  Instead of communicating like humans, we are using sophisticated bar-codes which only machines can read.  I seem to remember a similar concept, serial number tattoos, being very very bad.  But it's not just the code, it's the machines themselves and how they're ubiquitous.  The effect from 'smart phones' is two fold. Firstly, it allows the world's village idiots to unite and have collective power.  Before technology, villages knew to ignore and marginalize their local idiots.  Now, there are millions of them networked and connected having a lot of very real influence on the world.  Secondly, it generates more village idiots due to the dependency noted at the start.  That may sound a bit "mean", and so will this: Some people deserve to be marginalized.  Most people don't want someone with a sub-90 IQ to be their neuro-surgeon.  We also don't want them shaping laws that govern us, or influencing how businesses work on the whole, or a million other things in society.  It's not oppressive to say that no, little Johnny can't grow up to be a neuro-surgeon because he's 23 and still operating at a 6th grade level cognitively.  It's actually abusive  to entertain delusions that people can 'do anything', because they believe it, and they wind up getting their dreams dashed by reality.  Instead of realizing their limitations in a healthy way, people often feel constantly shut down by others, they externalize shortcomings(project them onto others instead of having humility).  These are ugly issues that may seem irrelevant, but cause/effect comes from or leads to a lot of places that we may find uncomfortable.  Not saying all technology is bad, but it has it's pros and cons.  We SHOULD be wary of our reliance on them, even when the science of how they work is very interesting.
Awesome technical overview! This was really cool!   Some fun info for marketers out there - some best practices to make your QR codes valuable to people.   Uses: - 'Learn More' links for the physical world where space is limited. Disclosures, ingredients, etc. - Transition from physical to digital space. Parking meter apps, ticket taking, payments, etc. Convenience, not awareness. - Reducing print needs. Restaurant menus for reduced germ transmission, maybe something where printing lots of things for lots of people might be needed, like an event program.  Guidelines: - Tell users what the QR does and where it came from. No 'scent of information' makes the code less trustworthy and enticing. Make clear what will happen when they use it "scan to pay" "donate now" "see the schedule" "see the map". "Learn more" is not enough. - Clarify which devices and apps can scan when necessary. This is less of a need than it used to be in the US. - Send users to mobile friendly pages. - Deep-link directly to relevant pages or actions. Don't make them navigate further. If it's an app, make clear the app is needed to take them there. Don't send them to a default homepage if they don't have the app. - Don't put a QR code on a page the user is intended to scan themselves. These are only useful for things like ticket agents, or users transitioning between two devices (tv app and mobile app). - Do not invert QR code colors. It should be black on white background. - QR codes must lead to up to date info. - If users have less than 15 seconds, use a short URL instead of a QR code. Many users will need longer than 15 seconds if they are uncertain, not comfortable with mobile devices, or have to dig it out of a pocket or bag. - Don't count on QR for frequent site visits. - Place QR codes where people are likely to look. They have to be easily noticed to be scanned. - Make sure they're big enough. While the official minimum size is 1x1 cm, a minimum of 2X2 cm is better. The farther away the user will be, the dimmer the lighting, the bigger you should make the code. Use a ratio of 10 to 1 for distance (50cm away, 5x5cm code).  Dangers of QR Codes - QR Codes have been used maliciously for phishing, malware, payments, tracking, often through fake codes applied over legit ones in public places.
Great video!  The future of QR codes is definitely color to move from binary encoding to base 5 or better (red, green, blue, black, white) to significantly increase data density for a given print layout.  Many of the design assumptions for the original QR format are overcome by events with the capabilities of modern phone cameras. We need an upgrade to Turbo Codes (no longer under patent) to further improve upon the Reed-Solomon coding and approach the theoretical limits of error detection/correction.  The static masks should be replaced with polynomial randomizers to further improve the clustered error correction (smudges and tears).  Lastly as mentioned it's to build in a public key infrastructure like X.509 similar to TLS 1.3 in HTTPS to ensure source validity and message security.  The last somewhat optional bit I would add is to move to move away from ASCII (English only) to unicode as a text encoding standard to support international languages/characters with an extendible code designator that would allow you to use potentially any encoding the users desire (beneficial for machine to machine).    If we did all of that QR codes would be firmly in place to ensure cheap, easy, passive information distribution across the global economy for the long haul.  Pairing two devices for any reason could then be done quickly, securely, and LOCALLY via that QR "this is me, this is how to connect" message.  I think QR codes will be essential to our need to significantly reduce product packaging and waste.  All of the essential labeling (nutrition, ingredients, source manufacturer, etc.) and marketing can be done via the QR code and then the packaging can be much simpler and most importantly reusable/recyclable.  Picture accessing your devices warranty and manual printed on the device as a QR code (such as in your cars door jam where the tire pressure is listed).  Medical records on a bracelet secured via encryption.  Credit cards replaced with one time use QR codes that encrypt the underlying messages so the info is useless if intercepted or stored.      The cost (and therefore delay) of the transition to a better QR will be on replacing all of the printer technology used in packaging.   Lucky for all of us as once the standard is updated the capability updates should come absolutely free to everyone's phone and could provide a lot of potential benefit and security enhancements.  I'm definitely glad you mentioned that the only safe way to use QR codes today is to actually LOOK at the URL details of where the link is trying to take you.  I've had to mention to more than a few restaurants that using QR codes with link shorteners makes it extremely dangerous for their customers especially when some of those sites allow online ordering and payment.
*A Deep Dive into QR Codes: From Morse Code to Modern Applications*  * *0:00** Intro and the Unexpected Rise of QR Codes:* The video explores the history and functionality of QR codes, challenging the initial skepticism towards their widespread adoption. * *0:42** Morse Code and the Roots of Digital Communication:* The story begins with Samuel Morse's quest for faster communication, leading to the development of Morse code – a system of dots and dashes representing letters and numbers. * *3:56** Barcodes and the Need for Efficiency:*  The invention of barcodes in the 1940s revolutionized the checkout process but had limited information storage capacity. * *8:09** Mad Cow Disease and the Limitations of Barcodes:* The need to track and trace information in the wake of mad cow disease highlighted the limitations of barcodes, leading to the exploration of 2D matrix codes. * *9:44** Masahiro Hara and the Go Board Inspiration:*  The video highlights the role of Masahiro Hara in developing the QR code, drawing inspiration from the Go board game to create a 2D code capable of storing more information. * *10:34** Building a QR Code on a Go Board:* The video demonstrates how to manually encode information into a QR code using a Go board, illustrating the conversion of text into binary data and its placement within the code structure. * *12:53** QR Code Structure: Position, Alignment, and Timing:*  The video explains the significance of position squares, alignment patterns, and timing strips in ensuring correct orientation and readability of the code. * *13:55** Data Format and Character Count:*  The video shows how the QR code indicates the data format (e.g., numeric, alphanumeric, byte) and the number of characters within the encoded message. * *18:14** Error Correction and Redundancy:* QR codes utilize Reed-Solomon error correction, allowing them to be read even when partially damaged. The video explains the concept of Hamming distance and how it relates to error correction capability. * *26:50** Masking for Optimal Readability:* Masking patterns are applied to QR codes to prevent large areas of uniform color, ensuring they are easily scanned by readers. * *29:55** The Open Patent and Smartphone Integration:*  The video attributes the success of QR codes partly to DENSO Wave's decision to make the patent open and the integration of QR code readers into smartphone camera apps. * *31:20** QR Code Safety and Potential Risks:*  The video addresses concerns about QR code safety, highlighting the importance of checking the destination URL before clicking on links accessed through QR codes. * *32:27** The Future of QR Codes and Limitless Possibilities:*  The video concludes by emphasizing the vast capacity of QR codes, making it virtually impossible to run out of unique codes.      I used gemini-1.5-pro-exp-0827 on rocketrecap dot com to summarize the transcript. Cost (if I didn't use the free tier): $0.03 Input tokens: 18896 Output tokens: 606
